blocks.markdown:
In **Compton scattering**, a photon scatters off a charged particle (usually an electron) and transfers part of its energy to it. The photon leaves with a longer wavelength. The wavelength shift depends only on the scattering angle:

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

The constant $\frac{h}{m_e c} \approx 2.43\,\mathrm{pm}$ is called the *Compton wavelength* of the electron.

blocks.question: Which particles participate in a Compton scattering process?
hints: One of the participants carries the electromagnetic wave.
The other participant is the lightest charged particle in an atom.

blocks.title: Wikipedia: Compton scattering
blocks.why: Read the introduction and the 'Description' section for the historical context of Compton's 1923 experiment.

blocks.question: According to the formula above, when is the wavelength shift Δλ the largest?
options: When the photon passes straight through (θ = 0°)
When the photon scatters at θ = 90°
The shift does not depend on the angle
hints: Look at the (1 − cos θ) factor: for which angle is it maximal?
cos 180° = −1, so the factor becomes 2 — its largest possible value.

blocks.markdown: Well done! You now know the basic kinematics of Compton scattering. In the next lesson we would derive the formula from conservation of **energy** and **momentum**, treating the photon as a particle with $E = h\nu$ and $p = h/\lambda$.
